---
title: "Generating Forward Models"
output: html_document
---

```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(assertthat)

base_dir <- "/projectnb/dietzelab/arober/pecan/modules/prob.tools"
code_dir <- file.path(base_dir, "R")
fwd_dir <- file.path(code_dir, "forward_model")

source(file.path(code_dir, "utils.r"))
source(file.path(code_dir, "types.r"))
source(file.path(code_dir, "batched_array.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "broadcast_rules.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "model_input.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_list.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_table.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_broadcast.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "run_model_api.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "forward_model_generators.r"))
```


## Introduction

In this case, each run requires a parameter vector as input, in addition
to some other information. 

```{r}
slot_list <- list(
  a = list(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6),
  b = list("seven", "eight", "nine"),
  c = matrix(7:14, nrow=4L) # Four different values of the parameter vector
)

ei <- EnsembleInputBroadcast(slot_list, 
                             list_rule = list(recycle = c("a", "b")))

# Summary of ensemble
summary(ei)

# Input indices for each run
print(ei)

# Looking at inputs for a particular run
input_slots(get_run_input(ei, "run_20"))
```


Suppose we want to generate a forward model that uses this ensemble run as 
the "run template"; that is, each execution of the forward model will run
the underlying model 24 times. Suppose also that we want to be able to pass in
the set of parameter vectors on the fly for each run. In other words,
slots `a` and `b` will be fixed, while slot `c` is free. We first define a toy
model.

```{r}
# Just print the input to visualize what is happening.
f <- function(x) {
  x <- input_slots(x)
  print(paste0(x$a, " - ", x$b, " - ", .get_vector_string(x$c)))
}
```


Now we can generate a "forward model", which returns a function that accepts
only the free slots as arguments. A "matrix forward model" always requires 
a matrix as input. The interpretation of this matrix is completely dependent
on the `EnsembleInput` template we defined previously. The forward model
generator by default prints a description of the forward model, which can
be silenced by setting `verbose = FALSE`.

```{r}
fwd <- wrap_partial_slot_batch(f, ei, slot_names="c")
```

Here's what the forward model looks like when printed. It is simply an R 
function that uses a closure, which allows the function to have a state 
determined by the `EnsembleInput` template, free slot names, and some other
information used for validating inputs to the forward model and using them
to set the values of the free slots.

```{r}
print(fwd)
```
Let's start by making sure the forward model behaves as expected by running it
at the existing value of `c` we set above, and comparing the result to a standard
call to `run_model_ensemble()`.


```{r}
output1 <- run_model_ensemble(f, ei)
```


```{r}
output2 <- fwd(slot_list$c)
```

```{r}
# Check that the two ensemble run outputs are the same.
all.equal(output1, output2)
```

The benefit of the forward model is that we now have a black-box function that
can easily be run at new parameter values without having to think about the 
`EnsembleInput`, model run API, etc.

```{r}
# Run forward model at new parameter values
new_c <- matrix(101:108, ncol=2L)
new_output <- fwd(new_c)
```

Trying to pass invalid inputs will result in errors.

```{r}
try(fwd(NULL)) # Non-array input
try(fwd(1:2))  # Incorrect dimension
```

# Other features

## Multiple Free Slots

We can also define a wrapped model where multiple slots are free. The 
function `wrap_partial_slot_batch()` imposes the requirement that the batch
size for these slots is the same. This allows the input to the wrapped model 
to be a matrix as before, where the number of rows is the common batch size
across the free slots. One row of this matrix represents one combination of 
values for all of the free slots, which have been concatenated into a single
vector.

```{r}
# Free slots (a,b,c) all have common batch size (2 in this case). Final slot 
# (d) is fixed. 
slot_list <- list(
  a = matrix(1:4, nrow=2L),
  b = matrix(1:10, nrow=2L),
  c = matrix(1:2, nrow=2L),
  d = list("a", "b", "c", "d")
)

ei <- EnsembleInputBroadcast(slot_list, 
                             list_rule = list(recycle = names(slot_list)))

# Summary of ensemble
summary(ei)

# Looking at inputs for a particular run
input_slots(get_run_input(ei, "run_2"))
```
```{r}
# Define the model.
f <- function(x) {
  out <- paste(.get_vector_string(x$a), .get_vector_string(x$b),
               .get_vector_string(x$c), x$d, sep=" | ")
  
  return(out)
}

# Test evaluation.
mi <- get_run_input(ei, "run_3")
f(mi)
```

```{r}
fwd <- wrap_partial_slot_batch(f, ei, slot_names=c("a", "b", "c"))
```

```{r}
# Verify the output is as expected.
out1 <- run_model_ensemble(f, ei)

free_slots_mat <- cbind(ei$slots$a, ei$slots$b, ei$slots$c)
out2 <- fwd(free_slots_mat)

all.equal(out1, out2)
```

```{r}
# Run at new values of input slots. 
new_vals <- matrix(1:16, nrow=2L)
fwd(new_vals)
```


## Output operator

Sometimes the model output is not needed directly, but rather some function
of the model output is desired. This could be defined manually, but the 
function `wrap_partial_slot_batch()` accepts an optional argument 
`output_operator` that serves this purpose. The `output_operator` is a function
that acts on the ensemble output list. In the example below, we define a 
toy output operator that simply concatenates all of the strings in the output list.

```{r}
# Define output operator.
output_op <- function(l) paste(l, collapse="\n")

# Generate model wrapper that includes output operator.
fwd_concat <- wrap_partial_slot_batch(f, ei, slot_names=c("a", "b", "c"),
                                      output_operator=output_op)

# Run model ensemble.
cat(fwd_concat(new_vals))

```

## Additional fixed arguments

Suppose the model `f` takes auxiliary arguments that are not included in 
`model_input`. One might wish to fix the values of these arguments when 
the model wrapper is created. This can be done with the `.fixed_args`
argument. These arguments must be named, and are immutable (they are fixed
over all calls to the wrapped model).

```{r}
# Add auxiliary argument to previous model.
f <- function(x, aux_arg) {
  out <- paste(.get_vector_string(x$a), .get_vector_string(x$b),
               .get_vector_string(x$c), x$d, aux_arg, sep=" | ")
  
  return(out)
}

# Pass auxiliary argument as fixed argument.
fwd_concat <- wrap_partial_slot_batch(f, ei, slot_names=c("a", "b", "c"),
                                      output_operator=output_op, 
                                      .fixed_args=list(aux_arg="auxarg"))

# Run ensemble model.
cat(fwd_concat(new_vals))

# Run ensemble model again with new model inputs. The fixed auxiliary argument
# will remain unchanged.
cat(fwd_concat(free_slots_mat))
```

## Dynamic Arguments

In other situations, a user might want an auxiliary argument to `f` to reflect
the current value of a variable in the calling scope of the wrapped model.
This can also be accommodated by passing the argument via `.dynamic_args`.
Dynamic arguments should be symbols or quoted objects. This can be useful 
when the wrapped model will be called repeatedly in some sort of iterative 
algorithm. We may want to treat the iteration number as a dynamic argument,
and use the iteration for some sort of logging or output path specification
within the model.

Below, we modify the previous example to feature both a fixed and dynamic argument.

```{r}
# Add another auxiliary argument to previous model.
f <- function(x, aux_arg, dyn_arg) {
  out <- paste(.get_vector_string(x$a), .get_vector_string(x$b),
               .get_vector_string(x$c), x$d, aux_arg, dyn_arg, sep=" | ")
  
  return(out)
}

# Treat the new auxiliary variable as dynamic.
fwd_concat <- wrap_partial_slot_batch(f, ei, slot_names=c("a", "b", "c"),
                                      output_operator=output_op, 
                                      .fixed_args=list(aux_arg="auxarg"),
                                      .dynamic_args=list(dyn_arg=quote(dyn_var)))

# Will throw error since `dyn_var` is not defined.
try(cat(fwd_concat(new_vals)))

# Specify value of the dynamic variable and run model ensemble.
dyn_var <- "dynvar"
cat(fwd_concat(new_vals))

# Run ensemble model again with new model inputs. Dynamic variable will be updated.
dyn_var <- "dynvarnew"
cat(fwd_concat(free_slots_mat))
```
